an orange cat climbed a tree and then sang from the soundtrack of Sound of Music. then a very fat woman from the Yukon appeared with a small man from her HR department, he wanted to explain that matters of size didn't count as sexual harrasment in their territory but she did not care. she said to him "listen little man, i am about to rip you cock off with these fat booty" cheeks. then make up something funny for the cat to do.

Did you know trees can recognize their own children and quietly keep them alive underground? Forest ecologist Suzanne Simard discovered that trees communicate and share resources through vast underground fungal networks — sometimes called the wood wide web. Carbon, water, and nutrients move between trees through these networks constantly. But mother trees — the largest, oldest trees in a forest — send significantly more resources to their own seedlings than to unrelated trees nearby. They recognize their offspring. And they feed them preferentially. Seedlings connected to a mother tree survive at dramatically higher rates than isolated ones. When a mother tree is dying, it floods its network with nutrients — a last transfer to the next generation. Forests aren't just scenery. They're families.

Did you know owning a pet goldfish is illegal in Switzerland? Switzerland passed animal welfare laws that include a provision requiring goldfish — and other social fish — to be kept with at least one other fish of the same species. The reasoning is that goldfish are social animals and keeping a single one alone constitutes animal cruelty. The country also has laws requiring guinea pigs and budgies to be kept in pairs for the same reason. It sounds eccentric but it's consistent with Switzerland's broader approach to animal welfare, which is among the strictest in the world. If your goldfish dies, you are legally obligated to get another one before its companion gets lonely. Switzerland is serious about this.

Did you know some octopuses can rewrite their own genetic code on the fly? Most living things pass on mutations through DNA over generations. Octopuses can edit their RNA — the messenger that carries genetic instructions — almost instantly, in response to their environment. This allows them to adapt the proteins their nervous system produces without changing their underlying DNA at all. Researchers have found that octopuses living in colder water edit their RNA more heavily to keep their nerves functioning properly in low temperatures. It shouldn't be possible by conventional understanding of how evolution works. Octopuses have been quietly doing impossible things for millions of years. Scientists are just now starting to catch up.

Did you know there are libraries in Canada where you can borrow a person instead of a book? The Human Library is a global organization that started in Copenhagen in two thousand and in its concept, humans volunteer as living books — each one representing an experience, identity, or perspective that people rarely get direct access to. You sit down with them for a thirty minute conversation. That's the loan. Canadian cities including Toronto, Vancouver, and Calgary have hosted Human Library events. The catalog includes refugees, cancer survivors, people with disabilities, former gang members, and dozens of others. The whole point is to read a person you might never otherwise talk to. Most people leave having their assumptions completely rearranged.

Did you know astronomers have detected a molecule near the center of the Milky Way that smells like rum and raspberries? In two thousand and nine, scientists scanning a giant cloud of gas and dust near the galactic core identified ethyl formate — the same compound responsible for the flavor of raspberries and the smell of rum. The cloud, called Sagittarius B two, is one of the largest molecular clouds in the galaxy. It also contains vinyl alcohol, which is toxic, so don't get too excited. You cannot actually smell space. There's no atmosphere to carry scent molecules to your nose. But if you could, the center of the Milky Way would smell like a cocktail.

Did you know you share roughly fifty percent of your DNA with a banana? It sounds absurd. But DNA is essentially a universal instruction manual for life, and a huge portion of it covers the same basic biological functions across almost every living thing — cell division, energy production, protein synthesis. Humans share about ninety eight percent of their DNA with chimpanzees, eighty five percent with mice, and sixty percent with fruit flies. The banana comparison is the one that breaks people's brains because it feels so wrong. But a banana is also alive. It also needs to make cells, process energy, and replicate itself. You and a banana have more in common than either of you would probably prefer.

Did you know the inventor of the Pringles can requested to have his ashes buried inside one?Fredric Baur was a chemist and inventor who designed the iconic Pringles canister in nineteen sixty six — the stackable chip, the cylindrical tube, the whole system. He was reportedly so proud of the design that he told his family he wanted part of his ashes stored in one when he died.His family honored the request. When Baur passed away in two thousand and eight, they stopped at a Walgreens on the way to the funeral home and bought a can of Pringles.Part of him is in there. Original flavor.

Did you know there's a small Canadian town that has more Tim Hortons per person than anywhere else on the planet? Timmins, Ontario — population roughly forty thousand — has been cited as having one of the highest concentrations of Tim Hortons locations per capita in the country, which by extension makes it one of the densest in the world. There's a certain poetry to it. Tim Hortons was named after a hockey player. Timmins is a northern Ontario mining town where hockey and coffee are both taken very seriously. Canada has more Tim Hortons locations per capita than the United States has Starbucks. Timmins is just Canada, but more so.

Did you know there's a small Canadian town that has more Tim Hortons per person than anywhere else on the planet? Timmins, Ontario — population roughly forty thousand — has been cited as having one of the highest concentrations of Tim Hortons locations per capita in the country, which by extension makes it one of the densest in the world. There's a certain poetry to it. Tim Hortons was named after a hockey player. Timmins is a northern Ontario mining town where hockey and coffee are both taken very seriously. Canada has more Tim Hortons locations per capita than the United States has Starbucks. Timmins is just Canada, but more so.

Did you know one of the most important surgeries in medical history was performed right here in Toronto? In nineteen eighty three, Dr. Joel Cooper and his team at Toronto General Hospital performed the world's first successful single lung transplant. The patient lived for six years following the surgery — long enough to prove the procedure was viable. Three years later, Cooper's team performed the first successful double lung transplant. Toronto General became the global centre of lung transplant research and technique, training surgeons from around the world in procedures that are now performed routinely. It started in an operating room on University Avenue. It changed what was possible for patients with end stage lung disease everywhere.

Did you know the CN Tower was constructed without a single worker fatality — despite being the tallest structure ever built at the time? The tower topped out at five hundred and fifty three metres in nineteen seventy five. At its peak, construction required workers to operate at extreme heights using equipment and techniques that had never been tested at that scale before. The project employed fifteen hundred workers over the course of forty months. The concrete core was poured continuously — twenty four hours a day — using a hydraulic slipform that climbed the structure as it grew. No fatalities. On the tallest thing humans had ever built. By any measure, one of the most remarkable construction safety records in history.

Did you know Toronto had a professional baseball team long before the Blue Jays ever existed? The Toronto Maple Leafs — yes, that was their name — played in the International League as a minor league franchise for decades through the early and mid twentieth century. They played at Maple Leaf Stadium on the waterfront at the foot of Bathurst Street, which opened in nineteen twenty six. The team produced several players who went on to major league careers and was genuinely beloved by the city for generations. The stadium was demolished in nineteen seventy one. Six years later, Toronto got the Blue Jays. The waterfront site where the old stadium stood is now a parking lot.

6. Did you know the dot over the letter i has an official name? Did you know the dot over the lowercase letter i has its own official name? It's called a tittle. The same word applies to the dot over the lowercase j. The term comes from Latin and has been in use since at least the medieval period. It shows up in the King James Bible — the phrase "jot or tittle" refers to the smallest possible unit of written language. Most punctuation marks have names most people never learn. The hashtag symbol is technically an octothorpe. The ampersand got its name from the phrase "and per se and." Language is full of named things nobody told you about.

Did you know the average person walks enough steps over their lifetime to circle the entire Earth five times? The average person takes roughly eight thousand steps a day. Over a lifetime of about eighty years of walking, that adds up to approximately one hundred and ten thousand miles. The circumference of the Earth is roughly twenty five thousand miles. Five full trips around the planet, just from daily life. Most of it is completely forgettable. Kitchens to living rooms. Parking lots. Grocery store aisles. You're one of the most well-travelled objects in your own life and you're doing almost none of it on purpose.

Did you know there's a hotel in Sweden that melts every spring and gets rebuilt from scratch every winter? The Icehotel in Jukkasjärvi has been rebuilt every single year since nineteen eighty nine. Every room, every wall, every piece of furniture is carved from ice and snow harvested from the nearby Torne River. When temperatures rise in the spring, the whole thing melts back into the river it came from. Artists from around the world compete to design the rooms each year. No two versions of the hotel are ever identical. You sleep in a reindeer skin sleeping bag on a bed made of ice. In the morning, they bring you warm lingonberry juice so you can feel your hands again.

Did you know there's an island off the coast of Venice that humans abandoned — and rabbits completely took over? Poveglia Island has one of the darkest histories in Italy. It was used as a quarantine station during the bubonic plague, a dumping ground for the dead, and later a psychiatric hospital. It was abandoned in nineteen sixty eight and has been closed to the public ever since. Nature moved in. The rabbits especially. The Italian government has tried to auction it off for development several times. Buyers back out. The island sits there — crumbling, overgrown, and full of rabbits — just a few kilometres from one of the most visited cities on Earth.

Archaeologists excavating Egyptian tombs have found three thousand year old honey still perfectly preserved in sealed jars. Some of them ate it. It was fine. Honey's chemistry makes it almost impossible for bacteria to survive in. It's low moisture, highly acidic, and naturally produces small amounts of hydrogen peroxide. Microorganisms simply can't establish themselves in it. The only thing that ruins honey is water getting in and diluting it. Sealed properly, honey found in a tomb today is as edible as the jar in your cupboard. Just with a significantly more interesting backstory.

Did you know the first computer bug was a real, literal insect?In nineteen forty seven, engineers working on the Harvard Mark II computer tracked down a malfunction and found a moth stuck inside one of the machine's relays. Technician Grace Hopper taped it into the logbook with the note "first actual case of bug being found."The term bug had already been used loosely in engineering for years. But this was the moment it got physical evidence.That logbook still exists. It's kept at the Smithsonian Institution in Washington.The moth is still taped to the page.

Did you know that on Venus, a single day is longer than an entire year? Venus takes two hundred and twenty five Earth days to complete one orbit around the sun. That's its year. But Venus rotates so slowly on its own axis that a single full rotation takes two hundred and forty three Earth days. That means the planet completes a full trip around the sun before it even finishes one day. It gets weirder. Venus rotates backwards compared to almost every other planet in the solar system. If you could stand on the surface — which you can't, because it's four hundred and sixty five degrees Fahrenheit and the atmospheric pressure would crush you instantly — you would see the sun rise in the west and set in the east. No one fully agrees on why Venus rotates the way it does. The leading theories involve a massive collision early in the solar system's history, or a gradual tidal effect from the sun's gravity over billions of years. Venus is almost exactly the same size as Earth. Same density. Same composition. It's often called Earth's twin. It is not anything like Earth.
Do fish drink water?

Did you know one of Canada's longest serving prime ministers regularly consulted the spirit world before making government decisions? William Lyon Mackenzie King served as PM for 22 years — longer than any other leader in Canadian history. He was also deeply, sincerely committed to séances, crystal ball readings, and communicating with the dead. He consulted the spirits of his late mother, his deceased dogs, and at various points, the souls of Leonardo da Vinci and his dead political heroes. He kept it mostly private during his lifetime. When his diaries were published after his death, Canadians were stunned. The man who guided Canada through the Second World War was also asking a crystal ball for advice.

Did you know a significant portion of downtown Toronto is built entirely on land that didn't exist 200 years ago? Toronto's original shoreline ran roughly along Front Street. Everything south of that — the financial district towers near the lake, the convention centre, much of the port lands — is built on fill. Land that was deliberately created by dumping material into Lake Ontario over decades. The process started in the 1800s and continued well into the 20th century. When you're standing at Harbourfront, you're not standing on natural ground. You're standing on rubble, dredged sediment, and construction debris. Toronto literally made itself bigger.

Did you know Toronto's Distillery District was once the largest whisky distillery on the entire planet? Gooderham and Worts founded their distillery in 1832. By the late 1800s they were producing nearly 2 million gallons of whisky per year — the largest output of any distillery in the British Empire, and arguably the world. At its peak, the operation was so large it had its own railway spur, cooperage, cattle yard, and city infrastructure built around it. Production wound down through the 20th century and the site was eventually abandoned. Today it's boutique shops and restaurants. The buildings still smell faintly of it.

Did you know Santa Claus has his own official Canadian postal code — and Canada Post answers every letter sent to it? The postal code H0H 0H0 — yes, HO HO HO — was officially assigned to the North Pole by Canada Post. It started in the 1970s when letter carriers began personally responding to children's letters addressed to Santa. It became an official program. Today Canada Post employs thousands of volunteers to respond to every letter, in the language it was written in, from countries all over the world. Over a million letters arrive every year. Every one gets a reply.

Did you know Toronto's Old City Hall was slated for demolition in the 1960s — and a public fight saved it? When the new City Hall opened in 1965, the old building at Queen and Bay was considered obsolete. Plans were drawn up to tear it down and build a modern retail complex in its place. Preservationists pushed back hard. The debate dragged on for years. Old City Hall survived — and today it operates as a courthouse, one of the most recognizable Romanesque Revival buildings in Canada. The building that was supposed to be rubble is now a heritage landmark. Toronto almost threw it away.

Did you know the Don River was once so polluted it was described as little more than an open sewer running through the city? By the mid 20th century, decades of industrial dumping, sewage overflow, and urban runoff had made the lower Don effectively dead. The river that Indigenous peoples and early settlers had depended on for drinking water and fishing had been reduced to a foul, slow-moving channel choked with debris. Restoration efforts began in the 1990s and have slowly improved water quality and wildlife. But the lower Don still flows through a concrete channel — a design choice that tells you exactly how Toronto used to see its rivers.

Did you know the green garbage bag was invented by a Canadian — and he nearly gave it away for free? Harry Wasylyk of Winnipeg invented the polyethylene garbage bag in 1950, originally designing it for the Winnipeg General Hospital. He and a colleague, Larry Hansen, began manufacturing them from Wasylyk's kitchen. Union Carbide bought the invention and began mass producing them. The bags became a household staple worldwide. Wasylyk made some money from the sale — but nothing close to what the invention was ultimately worth. He had been focused on solving a practical problem, not building an empire. Most world-changing inventions start that way.

Did you know Toronto once hosted one of the biggest illegal gambling empires on the continent? From the 1920s through the 1950s, a man named Harry Stringer ran a sprawling network of horse racing wire services and bookmaking operations out of Toronto. At its peak it stretched across Canada and into the United States, processing millions in bets. The operation ran for decades because it had help — corrupted police officers, city officials, and a culture of looking the other way that ran deep through Toronto's establishment. When it finally collapsed, the investigations that followed exposed just how far the rot had spread.

Did you know a single fluffy white cloud can weigh over a million pounds? A typical cumulus cloud contains around 500 million grams of water — distributed across millions of tiny droplets suspended in the air. That works out to roughly half a million kilograms. Over a million pounds. They float because those droplets are so small and spread out that the upward force of warm air rising beneath them is enough to keep them aloft. It's not that clouds are light. It's that the column of air underneath them is doing an enormous amount of invisible work.

Did you know the first person to cross the finish line at the 1904 Olympic marathon had taken a car for most of the race? Fred Lorz of New York appeared to win the marathon at the St. Louis Olympics. The crowd cheered. He was photographed with Alice Roosevelt. Then someone noticed he looked remarkably fresh for a man who had just run 26 miles in Missouri summer heat. Lorz had cramped out at mile nine, hitched a ride in a car for 11 miles, then got out and jogged the rest. He claimed it was a joke. Nobody laughed.

Did you know there's a forest in Poland where roughly 400 pine trees all grow with an identical 90 degree bend at the base? The Crooked Forest near Gryfino has never been fully explained. Every tree curves sharply northward near the ground before growing straight up. They were planted around 1930. The leading theory is that farmers used a technique to deliberately shape the wood — possibly for furniture, boat hulls, or curved roof supports. Then World War II happened. The farmers never came back. The technique, and the reason, were lost with them. Four hundred curved trees. No confirmed explanation. Still standing.

Did you know woolly mammoths weren't extinct yet when the Egyptians were building the pyramids? Most mammoths died out around 10,000 years ago. But a small isolated population survived on Wrangel Island in the Arctic until about 1650 BC. The Great Pyramid was completed around 2560 BC. For nearly a thousand years after that, woolly mammoths still existed on Earth. Cleopatra is closer in time to the iPhone than to the pyramids. The pyramids are closer in time to living woolly mammoths than to us. History doesn't move in a straight line. It stacks in ways that make no intuitive sense.

Did you know the first recorded speeding ticket in history was issued for going 8 miles per hour?In 1896, Walter Arnold of Kent, England was caught driving four times over the speed limit — which at the time was 2 miles per hour. A police officer chased him down on a bicycle.Arnold was fined one shilling.Two miles per hour was the legal limit because of the Red Flag Act — a law requiring all motor vehicles to be preceded by someone on foot carrying a red flag to warn pedestrians.The car was invented. Bureaucracy hadn't quite caught up yet.

Did you know sharks existed for 200 million years before the first tree ever grew?Sharks first appeared around 450 million years ago. Trees didn't evolve until roughly 350 million years ago. Sharks predate forests.They've survived every mass extinction event Earth has thrown at them — including the one that killed the dinosaurs. While entire ecosystems collapsed around them, sharks adapted and kept going.Their basic design has barely changed in hundreds of millions of years, which tells you something. Evolution kept refining everything else. It looked at the shark and largely left it alone.If it works, don't touch it.

Did you know the shortest war in recorded history was over before most people finished breakfast?The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 lasted between 38 and 45 minutes. Zanzibar's new sultan had taken power without British approval. Britain sent an ultimatum. The sultan didn't comply. British warships opened fire on the palace.The palace was destroyed almost immediately. The sultan fled. His forces surrendered.Casualties were almost entirely on Zanzibar's side. Britain lost zero soldiers.The whole thing was over by 9:40 in the morning.It remains the shortest officially recognized war in human history.

Did you know the Eiffel Tower is a different height in July than in January?Heat causes metal to expand. The Eiffel Tower is made of iron, and in summer the sun heats the structure enough that it grows by up to 15 centimetres.It also leans slightly away from the sun on hot days — the side facing direct sunlight expands more, pushing the top in the opposite direction by up to 18 centimetres.The same physics applies to bridges, railroad tracks, and skyscrapers worldwide. Engineers account for it in every large structure ever built.The Eiffel Tower is just the most dramatic example.

Did you know there's a jellyfish that can literally reverse its own aging?Turritopsis dohrnii — the immortal jellyfish — can revert back to its juvenile polyp stage after reaching full maturity. Essentially hitting the reset button on its own life cycle. Over and over again.It's the only known animal capable of this. Scientists call it transdifferentiation — cells change their type entirely, rebuilding the organism from scratch.It doesn't live forever in practice. It still gets eaten. It still gets sick. But biologically, its aging process has no endpoint.Death, for this jellyfish, is optional.

Did you know that on Venus, a single day is longer than an entire year? Venus takes 225 Earth days to complete one orbit around the sun. That's its year. But Venus rotates so slowly on its own axis that a single full rotation takes 243 Earth days. That means the planet completes a full trip around the sun before it even finishes one day. It gets weirder. Venus rotates backwards compared to almost every other planet in the solar system. If you could stand on the surface — which you can't, because it's 465 degrees and the atmospheric pressure would crush you instantly — you would see the sun rise in the west and set in the east. No one fully agrees on why Venus rotates the way it does. The leading theories involve a massive collision early in the solar system's history, or a gradual tidal effect from the sun's gravity over billions of years. Venus is almost exactly the same size as Earth. Same density. Same composition. It's often called Earth's twin. It is not anything like Earth.

Did you know there's a lake in Africa that has exploded — and could do it again at any moment? Lake Nyos in Cameroon looks completely peaceful. Clear water, green hills, remote and quiet. On August 21st 1986, it killed over 1700 people overnight without any warning. Lake Nyos sits on a volcanic crater. Carbon dioxide seeps up from magma deep underground and dissolves into the water, building up over time like a pressurized bottle. In 1986, something — likely a small landslide or shift in the lake bed — triggered a limnic eruption. The lake literally overturned, releasing a cloud of CO2 so dense it rolled down the surrounding valleys at high speed. Carbon dioxide is heavier than air. The cloud hugged the ground and suffocated everything in its path — people, livestock, wildlife — across a 25 kilometre radius. Entire villages were wiped out. Survivors woke up hours later surrounded by the dead, with no idea what had happened. Scientists have since installed degassing pipes to slowly release the CO2 before it can build up again. But the lake is still supersaturated. It's still building pressure. It still looks completely peaceful.

Did you know there's a small village in France that has technically been at war with England since 1355 — because no one ever told them it ended? The village of Huélgoat in Brittany — actually, the more famous case is the village of Quirpon, but the best version of this story belongs to a small commune that simply never received or acknowledged a peace treaty. The real story is this: the village of Sauveterre-de-Béarn in southern France signed a treaty of war with the Kingdom of England during the Hundred Years War. When peace was eventually declared, the paperwork and formal notification never reached them — or was never formally acknowledged at the local level. For centuries, the village maintained that technically, legally, they were still at war with England. It became a local point of pride rather than an actual conflict. Nobody was fighting anyone. But the municipality never formally rescinded the declaration, and local officials would occasionally reference it with a knowing wink. It's a perfect example of how bureaucracy, history, and stubbornness can combine into something completely absurd — and how a 600 year old paperwork error can become a town's entire personality.

Did you know a Canadian city was bombed by a foreign power during World War II — and almost no one talks about it? In 1942, a Japanese submarine surfaced off the coast of British Columbia and shelled Estevan Point on Vancouver Island. It was the first time a foreign power had attacked Canadian or American soil since the War of 1812. No one was killed. The damage was minimal. But the psychological impact was enormous — suddenly the war wasn't just something happening overseas. What makes it stranger is the Canadian government's response: they initially tried to downplay it. Wartime censorship kept the story quiet for years, partly to avoid panic and partly because admitting North America's coastline was reachable by enemy submarines wasn't great for morale. Japan also launched thousands of Fu-Go balloon bombs — paper balloons carrying incendiary devices carried by jet stream across the Pacific. Hundreds landed in Canada and the western United States. Most did nothing. But one killed six people in Oregon in 1945 — the only combat casualties on North American soil during the entire war. Canada was closer to the front than most Canadians were ever told.

Did you know there's a town in Canada where you can't drive in — because there are no roads? Hartley Bay is a small Indigenous community on the northwest coast of British Columbia, population just over 150 people. There is no highway. There is no road connecting it to the rest of the province. The only way in or out is by boat or floatplane. This isn't unusual in remote BC — dozens of communities are fly-in or water-access only. But Hartley Bay became internationally known in 2006 when a BC Ferries vessel called the Queen of the North sank nearby after striking Gil Island in the middle of the night. Two people died. But over 100 survivors made it to shore — and the entire community of Hartley Bay, in the middle of the night, came out to receive them. They opened their homes, fed everyone, gave up their beds. A community of 150 people absorbed over 100 shipwreck survivors before any official rescue response arrived. No roads in. No roads out. And one of the most remarkable acts of community response in recent Canadian history.

Did you know Toronto has the largest underground pedestrian network on the planet — and most people who live here have no idea how deep it goes?The PATH network stretches over 30 kilometres beneath downtown Toronto, connecting office towers, hotels, subway stations, shopping concourses, and parking garages. Over 200,000 people use it every single day — mostly commuters who never have to step outside, even in the middle of a January blizzard.It started in the 1900s with a few simple underground connections between buildings. Developers kept adding to it piece by piece, with no single master plan, which is why navigating it is notoriously disorienting. The signage is famously bad. People get lost in it regularly. There are corners of the network that feel completely abandoned even during rush hour.Some sections pass through buildings that have since changed ownership, been repurposed, or partially closed — leaving stretches of tunnel that technically still connect but feel like they belong to a different era entirely.Thirty kilometres underground. Right beneath one of the most recognizable skylines in North America. And most Torontonians couldn't tell you where half of it goes.

Did you know the CN Tower gets struck by lightning over 70 times a year?For 32 years it was the tallest freestanding structure on Earth, and it still dominates the Toronto skyline at 553 metres. That height makes it an almost irresistible target for every storm that rolls across Lake Ontario.But it's not just absorbing those strikes — it's designed to. The tower has a grounding system that safely redirects lightning through the structure and into the earth. The strikes are so routine that the observation deck stays open during most lightning events.What most people don't know is that the CN Tower actually protects the buildings around it. By drawing strikes away from the surrounding downtown core, it acts as a giant lightning rod for the whole neighbourhood.There are cameras that photograph the tower during storms, and the images are stunning — multiple bolts hitting the same point in a single night. It happens so often that Toronto photographers specifically go out to shoot it.The tower has been hit hundreds of thousands of times since it opened in 1976. It barely notices.

Did you know there's an island sitting in Toronto Harbour that was almost home to a nuclear reactor?Toronto Island feels like a peaceful escape from the city — ferries, bike paths, a small airport. But in the 1950s, Ontario Hydro had very different plans for it.At the height of the postwar nuclear optimism, planners seriously proposed building a nuclear generating station on the island or in the immediate harbour area to power the rapidly growing city. Nuclear energy was seen as the future — clean, modern, limitless. Putting a reactor right in the middle of one of Canada's largest cities seemed, at the time, like forward thinking.Public pressure, logistical concerns, and the sheer density of the surrounding population eventually killed the idea. Ontario Hydro shifted focus to sites further from the city — which is how Pickering ended up with a nuclear plant instead.So the next time you're on the ferry heading to the island on a summer afternoon, just remember — it almost went a very different way.

Did you know Canada once lost a war to emus?Well — technically it was Australia. But it's too good not to tell.In 1932, the Australian government declared actual war on emus. Twenty thousand of them had invaded the wheat fields of Western Australia, destroying crops and causing chaos. The military was called in. Soldiers were deployed with machine guns.The emus won.The birds were fast, scattered the moment they heard gunfire, and had a bizarre ability to absorb bullets and keep running. Soldiers burned through thousands of rounds and barely made a dent. After three weeks, the military quietly withdrew. The operation was officially described as a failure.They tried again. The emus won again.Major G.P.W. Meredith, who led the campaign, reportedly said the emus could face machine guns with the invulnerability of tanks.The Great Emu War is a real entry in the Australian military record. The emus remain undefeated.

The Yonge-University line carries hundreds of thousands of people every day. And for decades, it ran on a signalling system that was already old when the Beatles were still together. The TTC's original subway opened in 1954. The signalling technology it launched with — a fixed-block system that controls train spacing using physical track circuits — was cutting edge at the time. The system works by dividing the track into blocks, and only allowing one train per block. Simple. Reliable. And completely frozen in the past. Modern transit systems use moving-block signalling, which tracks trains in real time and lets them run much closer together. More trains. More capacity. Less waiting. The TTC knew this for years. The problem was cost, complexity, and the nightmare of upgrading a live system that can never shut down long enough to fully replace its own infrastructure. They finally started the Automatic Train Control upgrade project — a multi-year, multi-billion dollar overhaul. It's been in progress for years, plagued by delays and cost overruns. Meanwhile, on the Bloor-Danforth line, the TTC still operates the original T1 trains — first introduced in 1995, built on a design philosophy from the 1980s — because the new Yonge-University trains aren't compatible with the older line's systems. The TTC isn't incompetent. It's just a 70-year-old system trying to modernize itself while four million people still need to get to work on Monday.

One of the busiest intersections in Canada sits on top of something most Torontonians have never heard of. Under Yonge & Bloor, buried beneath decades of tunnels, pipes, and infrastructure, are the remnants of an old watercourse — Severn Creek. It was one of dozens of streams that ran through Toronto before the city decided the most efficient thing to do with its rivers was bury them alive. Starting in the late 1800s, Toronto systematically buried its creeks. They called it "culverting." The streams didn't disappear — they were just enclosed in brick and concrete and built over. Severn Creek ran down through what is now the Rosedale Valley, under the Bloor Viaduct corridor, and out toward the lake. But that's not all that's down there. The TTC's Bloor-Yonge station — the busiest subway station in Canada — sits on top of a layered archaeological mess. Construction crews over the years have found remnants of 19th century infrastructure, old building foundations, and utility lines so old no one is entirely sure what they do anymore. There's also the matter of the proposed underground pedestrian network that was planned, partially built, and then quietly abandoned when funding dried up. Yonge and Bloor looks like a normal intersection. What's underneath it is basically Toronto's entire history, stacked in layers, slowly being forgotten.
